speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Today is December 22nd, 1995. We are in the home of court reporter from the Nuremberg Trials, Vivien Spitz, maiden name, Putty. My name is Gary Lubell. I will be the interviewer today. And we are in Aurora, Colorado, United States of America. And we'll be conducting this interview in English. Oh, slate again?

speaker: interviewer
text: Just-- you know, you could-- you can drop that. Just tell us your name and--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. My name is Gary Lubell. I will be the interviewer today. We're in Aurora, Colorado, United States of America, conducting this interview in English, with Nuremberg court reporter, Vivien Spitz. And today is December 22, 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think I did the same thing backwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready to roll?

speaker: interviewer
text: You can speak. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Good morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Gary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like to start with where you were born. And-- and also, if you could tell me your birth date, and your full name, and spelling, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is July 21, 1924. And I was born in Anaconda, Montana, when my parents were traveling from Seattle to Joliet, Illinois, for a new position for him. And I was born on the way, on this trip. So we had to make a stop off in Anaconda, Montana. That's how I happened to be born there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me, if you will, your parents' names.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Anna Catherine, with a C, von Eresch, E-R-E-S-C-H. My father was Guy Vivien, V-I-V-I-E-N, Putty, P-U-T-T-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were your parents born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was born in Beloit, Kansas, in 1894. And my father was born in Hopkinsville, Kentucky, the same year.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And do you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have one brother living, John Kenneth Putty, in Dallas, who is-- who is now 65. And my sister died in 1978 at age 52.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Edna Mae, M-A-E, Putty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are some of your earliest memories from your childhood? Where did you grow up, and what do you recall from early childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember growing up in Joliet-- I mean, for most of that young-- very young period of time-- in Joliet, Illinois. My father was a professional chef. And he worked at the Silver Cross Hospital, in Joliet. And that's where I went to-- my early years in school. It was a very difficult period, during the Depression, for my family. I just don't remember much other than going to grade schools, elementary schools, in Joliet, Illinois--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --as a very young child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say that it was a difficult time during the Depression. What memories do you have of that? What-- what were some of the difficulties your family faced?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- my father was an alcoholic. And he had difficulty being on a job for a very long period of time. He was a top chef at country clubs and hospitals. And so this made life very difficult. My mother and father both had a-- a rabbit tree, which is what it was called at that time. And they raised prize rabbits, which brought them not only three-foot high trophies, but additional income. They also raised-- we had a dog kennel of Boston Terrier-- the little Boston Terrier bulldogs. And they were American Kennel Club registered, so this also brought an additional-- my mother was a rock who really held our family kind of steady for a-- a substantial period of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little more about your mother, if you will.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was the German in-- in my family. And she was-- she was born in Beloit, Kansas, and educated at the college at Lawrence, Kansas. She, in her pre-marriage years, was a medical records librarian at the Karl Menninger Institute and-- in Topeka, I believe that is-- and actually worked with the old, original of the old Dr. Karl Menninger, during those years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say her heritage was German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dating back to-- at what point in time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her grandparents were born in Germany. Their name was Groeppengeizer, G-R-O-E-P-P-E-N-G-E-I-Z-E-R, or S-E-R. I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they settled in-- eventually-- early on in the German community, in Aurora, Illinois, and then on to Kansas, where her father had a-- a wheat farm in Kansas, until his death. My mother and father-- my my mother's father and mother separated at age 1 for her. So she was actually raised in Kansas by her father. And then they were reunited again when she was 21, with her mother. It was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: What about on your father's side? Do you-- do you know your family tree on that side?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's family came initially from England. And his mother and father, in Hopkinsville, Kentucky, had a tobacco farm, a very large tobacco farm. And his grandparents actually had slaves in the same town-- maybe about a dozen slaves. And he had-- I-- I never really knew much of that family. We visited early on, occasionally, but very infrequently, with that side of the family. So an uncle and an aunt that I knew and had met, I never really knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: What religion was your family? And-- and tell me a little bit, if you will, about your religious background and training.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a-- a non-practicing, if you will, Southern Baptist. And my mother was Catholic-- Roman Catholic. And so my father would not allow my mother to have me baptized at birth in the Catholic church. So it was not until I was a teenager, actually, not long before his death, that-- that I was baptized. I went to two public schools. I went to Baptist Sunday schools in my very, very young life. And in high school, in Woodstock, Illinois, I went to a Catholic church, but I never attended any Catholic schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say that your father passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when I was 17. He became such a serious alcoholic. Severe alcohol like that, he-- by that time we were living in Woodstock, Illinois, just a small town-- 6,000 people-- just an hour and a 1/2 northwest of Chicago. And he was then a chef at-- at what was then called an old people's home, in Woodstock. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I've lost my thought there. It was at-- when I was 17, that he killed himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How did he do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had been-- it was a little town. And we-- my mother, when life was very difficult, would call the local police. And they would just come and pick him up and put him in the jail there to sober up. And that was pretty much-- he was literally the town drunk-- and the only one. And at this one time, he was-- it was an evening-- and he-- well, they took him out of his cell to play poker with them-- the fire chief, the police chief, somebody else there, and my father. And then there was a fire in the town somewhere, and they all had to go to take care of this fire problem and put him back in his cell until they returned, at which time they were going to continue the card game. And it was during the time they were gone that he used his belt to hang himself in the cell.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you remember this affecting your family, and-- and you, particularly?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- well, I-- I was really-- I felt very glad, if you will, because I had spent 17 years of a young life with the-- he was very, very brutal. And I know that the three of us children, did not-- we really did not have any kind of love for a father because of the brutality which was visited upon our mother, predominantly. And as much as I wanted her to just leave him and get us out of this situation, she was not really able to do it. And she just went to work to provide income in the Woodstock typewriter factory. And it-- it-- it just seemed as though after his death, our life started-- literally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that point you were 17 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had-- I was just graduating from high school. And we did not have the money. I did not have the money. Nor did my mother, from the-- the limited income that she received from her father's wheat farm in Kansas. She had a tenant farmer there. But there was not money for me to go to college. I lived this far out from Chicago. And she borrowed enough money on her wheat farm for me to-- to go into Chicago, to Gregg Reporting College. I-- it was my intention, along with some of my other college-- I mean, high school seniors-- to go in-- who were not going into college prep courses in the university somewhere-- to go into Chicago for the secretarial course at Gregg College-- Gregg Business College-- which had a very extensive, highly-regarded court reporting course. It was never my intention to be a court reporter. I didn't know what a court reporter was. But I had a high school teacher named Ellis Alter, who always had wanted to be a court reporter. And unbeknownst to me, he made me his alter ego while I was in college. And he knew the difficulties in our personal life, in our family. And, I, of course, studied shorthand under him. And he made a point of keeping me after school every day. School ended at 3:30, and I went across the street to work on the switchboard in the hospital at 5:30 every day. And I had two hours of dead time. So he kept me after school, and he worked me up to a speed of 140 words a minute. Now, you know, at that time, secretaries were graduating from high school at 80 words a minute-- 100 if they were really good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he got me up to that speed. And I didn't know what he had in mind. I knew that I had enough money to go into Chicago from my mother's loan, for three months, which I did. And, of course, when I went into Chicago to register at Gregg College, I did not know what my teacher knew. And that is that the secretarial course ended at 140 words a minute. So there was only one place for me to go, and that was into the court reporting course for three months. And I went in every morning on the 400 Northwestern train at 7:00 AM, at this little, tiny town train station. And I got into Chicago at 8:30, and into Union station; walked two blocks to get a bus to go to Michigan Avenue, and then off the bus; and walked two blocks to Gregg College, which was on Michigan Avenue-- number six, Michigan Avenue.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, I was really interested in that court reporting course. After three months, I was well into the course and gaining speed. And every night, I had to travel back out to Woodstock. The exciting thing about those train trips was that in our little town of Woodstock also lived Chester Gould, who drew Dick Tracy. And we got on the same train every morning and the same train every night. And he would let me sit with him while he pulled out his art frame, and would draw another frame for Dick Tracy. At any rate, that made the trip a little bit easier-- less time for studying, because I was so interested in his art. At-- however, after three months, my mother borrowed more money, and more money. And I knew that this was a two year course. And I finished it. I managed to finish the course and pass the required 200 words per minute in shorthand-- Gregg shorthand-- reporting shorthand-- not secretarial shorthand, which is a very abbreviated system-- in 13 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it, do you think, about you that your high school business teacher saw in you that-- that made him choose to be so-- oh-- so much of a-- an advocate for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. Maybe I showed the ability to gain greater speed early on in the teaching of manual shorthand. We don't even teach it anymore, and we haven't for years-- maybe 35 or 40 years-- 30 years certainly, at least, at that time-- right about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you this. What was your knowledge at that time? Because in-- in-- the years are-- I guess we're talking about the late 30s, early 40s-- when you're going through this experience. Is that-- is that accurate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I was in high school from 1938 to 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your knowledge at this time about what was taking place in Eastern Europe and in that part of the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very minimal. Of course, the only paper we had was the in this little town. And my life was so difficult working to bring in money from that hospital job for-- to supplement my mother's income, and my-- my brother-- my younger brother and sister in school. And it just seemed, you know, that that was all that I knew-- all that we knew-- in-- in those days, in that high school. High school activities-- I was involved in the artwork in-- which was an interest of mine, and worked on the school newspaper those years, and also a little bit of drama, and in the plays that the school put out. But out-- in the outside world was non-existent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your sense of current events of the time was based on what was happening within your own experience, in your own world--

speaker: interviewee
text: Within that community, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --within the community. Let me ask you this. Within the community itself, was there some diversity? Were there people of different religions, races? What was your awareness about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an interesting little town built on a square-- and still is. And with-- that square is on the National Historic register. There was one Jewish family, the Wien family, W-I-E-N, who had a beautiful ladies' clothing store; gorgeous daughter who went to high school with us; and one black family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how large was the community? How many-- how many people would you say lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Woodstock? 6,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: About 6,000-- 1 Jewish family, and 1 black family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one black family. We also had a-- a prominent Italian family, the Stompanado family, who had the beauty shop and the men's barber shop-- the only one in town. And it was from that family, John Stompanado, a son, who became a member of the mafia, who killed-- let me see-- Lana Turner's-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no-- Cheryl-- the daughter-- Lana Turner's boyfriend, I guess, in the bedroom. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Interesting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we also had, in that town, Orson Welles, who was in a private school for boys there. And that name slips my mind. But it was a prominent school. Orson Welles had been-- was an only child in a prominent Chicago dentist family. And both his mother and father were killed in an automobile accident. So friends put him in this school, in Woodstock. So we had these interesting people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not know him personally, but saw him continually hanging around the-- the Coke and ice cream joint. And Todd School for Boys was the name of the school. That's where he developed his--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you this. What values were-- let's see, how do I want to phrase this? What were you taught as a child about people that were different from yourself? What-- what did your family, your parents, share with you, or perhaps, through the teachings of the church, about people who are different than yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember anything, particularly. I know that my father was-- because he had been born and raised in the South, was anti-black. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I do not remember anything about bigotry or being taught anything. I can remember, somewhere along the way, I became very conscious of the black problem-- my father's comments. And that just seemed to resonate in-- with me. And I-- I resisted it. But I don't remember anything. And, of course, I did not go to a Catholic school-- the church in that town. The-- of course, the homilies always followed the-- or the-- and and But as far as values and the differences between peoples-- ethnic differences, racial, religious-- I don't remember anything, particularly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother very open about her feelings towards people that were different?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember it ever coming up.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She certainly did not support my father and whatever negative comments he had to make about blacks. No, she didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship like with your brothers? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother and sister, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother and sister, I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was three years younger than I was. And it was just a-- a sibling relationship-- I guess, ordinary. We were only three years apart in school, as we went through school. My brother was six years younger. And he-- and I felt so close to him. I felt especially close to him, and maybe in a protective way, too. Because it was difficult for him with this kind of father that we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a strong connection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, between my brother and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And with you and your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- we were just sisters. I don't remember anything particularly joyous about our [LAUGHS] relationship as we grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, let's go back to the court reporting school. And you got to the end of three months in school. What-- what actually took place at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, I-- I went into that school at 140 words per minute, when other students who were going into the court reporting course may have not had the push behind them that I had with this high school teacher. After three months, I was writing 175 words a minute. We also had all of the business courses that went along with the-- the manual skill of writing shorthand at very high speed. We had to reach 200 words a minute on different kinds of tests-- court-- that would be found in the courtroom testimony-- jury, charge, and literary. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It seemed as though by the time I was at 175 words per minute-- and we had a heavy medical terminology schedule in that school-- that I just had to go on-- had to find the way. And it was-- it seems as I-- as though-- I remember $30 a month it cost to go in and out on the train. And every three month period was $90 a month more, which was a great sum of money to my family at that time-- and then the tuition at the school. This worried me all the time. I have to get through this. I've got to do this faster, because of that. So my mother got the money, and we extended it for another three months. And the three months went to another three months. Fortunately, she had that farm in Kansas. And after 13 months, I passed my-- my high speed test at 200 words per minute, which was the graduating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what would have been the normal time that-- that somebody would go through a course like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years-- it was a two-year course. And I have a very good friend, even to this day, a court reporter in Omaha, Nebraska, who was in the school. And I went in, and he was there quite a while after I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And he--

speaker: interviewee
text: We laugh about that today.

speaker: interviewer
text: He probably still wonders how you did it in 13 months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Where did you go from there, Vivien?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three job offers immediately-- one in Chicago, and one in Sioux Falls, South Dakota, and one in Detroit. And I knew after traveling in those snow blizzards down the canyons in downtown Chicago, that I did not want to work there. And I thought about how nice it would be-- Sioux Falls, South Dakota. But I knew there was a military base there. And I somehow had a negative feeling about like, military bases and-- and going to work there. So I went to Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you think that negative feeling towards the bases came from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really don't know, unless that was-- unless it was through publicity in the paper, maybe, about GIs and-- and the-- I-- I don't remember anything particularly negative. But it was a-- it was a GI town, and I wasn't sure that's where I wanted to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you decided to go to Detroit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of job did you take on there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took a job with a freelance court reporting agency there, meaning that we worked in-- on assignment in the court-- courts of Detroit-- in taking depositions, which were eventually used in court proceedings; reporting conventions, such as a Bar Association convention. And our office had the contract with the Detroit Police Department to report every single incident that happened during the night in Detroit. This was within a short-- let's see-- 1943 was the race riot-- 1943. Just after that, I went to Detroit. So there was this heavy tension in that city-- race-- a racial tension, and I was very aware of that. The prosecutor's contract required us to be on call two nights a week-- each one of us reporters. No matter what happened in that city from a-- a fairly bad accident to the worst murder, we would get a phone call from the prosecutor's office in the middle of the night to get to the prosecutor's office, because they would not proceed without a court reporter there. I have-- I've never known of such a thorough system anywhere. And I can remember I lived in a large, beautiful boarding house up off of Woodward Avenue, across from the art museum, in a wonderful area of the city. And I would get my calls at 2:00 or 3:00 in the morning. And I remember one night, Saturday night, I had a call from the prosecutor's office that they had a terrible murder.
